frameworks.name: Want Less or Trade More
frameworks.definition: A two-option resolution to the dissatisfaction of wanting two incompatible things: either reduce your desire for the thing you say you want, or pay the cost required to get it. There is no third path.

frameworks.name: Active Active, Passive Passive
frameworks.definition: A capital management rule: keep passive investments managed by partners so they consume no founder attention, and deploy active effort only in the core business where ROI on time is highest.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that regret is generated not by choosing wrong paths but by mentally picturing the upside of an unchosen path without simultaneously imagining the downside cost that path would have required. The imagined upside with no imagined cost makes the unchosen path look better than any real alternative actually is.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: The mind isolates the upside of a path not taken without pairing it with the suffering that path would have demanded, creating a phantom comparison that no real option can match.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that the dissatisfaction of wanting both options simultaneously (e.g., both family time and $100M growth) is not a problem to be solved but a preference to be clarified: either want less of one thing or consciously pay the cost to get the other. There is no path that delivers both without accepting a trade.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
bounded_by: Applies to genuinely incompatible goals, not to goals that can be sequenced

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that for a roofing/exterior remodeling owner at $6M trying to reach $100M without increasing personal working hours, the path requires accepting a short-term reduction in profitability in exchange for the high-caliber talent who can lead the growth on the founder's behalf.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Founder wants to reach $100M without adding personal working hours
Business is in exterior remodeling or a comparable skilled-trade service
claims.mechanism: High-level talent commands premium compensation; accepting lower short-term profit margins is the trade that purchases time freedom.
bounded_by: Requires that the founder has the character and vision that would attract high-level executives; does not work if neither is present

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that to attract high-level executive talent, a founder must have two things: the character (reliability, integrity) that the talent would want to follow, and a vision large enough that the executive's own ambitions can fit inside it.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Recruiting executives who have non-financial alternatives

claims.text: Alex Hormozi describes a progression through hiring levels where each step reveals how much better the previous level was missing, with approximate annual compensation anchors at $50k, $100k, $250k, $500k, $1M, and multi-million dollars. Each level unlock produces a qualitative jump in capability that makes the prior level look comparatively limited.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Scaling service businesses that move from owner-operator to executive-led management
claims.mechanism: Each compensation tier selects for a qualitatively different set of skills, judgment, and network; the jump is not linear but reveals a capability category that the lower tier cannot simulate.

numbers.axis: annual employee compensation as a proxy for talent level

bounded_by: Compensation alone does not guarantee capability; fit and role clarity still matter

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that the best talent available to a founder is always in the future, not the past; whatever quality of hire exists today, superior talent remains available ahead and has not yet been recruited.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that for entrepreneurs with multiple side businesses (real estate, other ventures), those investments are only a distraction if they actively consume founder attention and time; held passively with capable managers, they are not a distraction to the core business.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Passive investments are managed by competent partners with no required founder involvement
bounded_by: Becomes a distraction the moment the founder begins to actively manage or optimize the passive investment

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that passive capital deployed into REITs, funds, or partner-managed real estate should be kept strictly passive because the return on founder time in the core business vastly exceeds the incremental return from actively managing passive investments.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Founder has a high-growth core business with large remaining upside
claims.mechanism: Active management of passive investments adds a time cost that exceeds the incremental return improvement, while founder time in the core business compounds at a much higher rate per hour.
bounded_by: Does not apply if the founder has no high-growth active business to deploy time into

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that a founder has approximately four to five large high-effort business seasons remaining in their productive lifetime, making each season an irreplaceable asset that limits the total number of big bets available.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Applies to founders in mid-career with roughly 30–40 productive years remaining
claims.mechanism: Each big bet requires enough time to become genuinely excellent at something; given how long mastery takes, the number of pursuits that can be completed at a high level within one career is small.
bounded_by: Season length varies by industry and individual; 4–5 is an order-of-magnitude heuristic, not a precise count

claims.text: Alex Hormozi uses Warren Buffett's 20-punch-card analogy to argue that entrepreneurs who treat each business opportunity as consuming one of a fixed number of punches will make dramatically better investment and focus decisions than those who treat opportunities as unlimited.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: Finite-resource framing forces higher quality filtering; when each investment cannot be undone and the total number is capped, the decision standard rises dramatically.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that for a founder at $6M revenue, the fear of losing family time during a push toward $100M is a psychological barrier, not a logistical inevitability, because the growth path can be structured through high-level talent rather than additional founder hours.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Founder is willing to sacrifice short-term profit margins to fund the talent required
bounded_by: Requires that the founder can attract and retain executive-level talent; not possible if the business lacks attractive vision or founder character

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that the cost of pursuing one big goal is the ongoing sacrifice of all new ideas and exciting side pursuits; people who keep adding side pursuits are implicitly preferring the stimulation of novelty over the compounding returns of singular focus.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: Maintaining singular focus requires continuously declining new opportunities; the cognitive and emotional energy that goes into evaluating, onboarding, and managing new ventures is the direct cost extracted from the core pursuit.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that short-term profit and short-term family time cannot both be maximized simultaneously during a scale-up, but family time lost in the short term cannot be recovered in the long term, whereas profit foregone in the short term can be recovered through later earnings.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Founder is in an active scale-up phase with young children or time-sensitive family relationships
bounded_by: Profit recoverability depends on the business remaining viable and the market remaining available in the long term

examples.text: Alex Hormozi used the analogy of wanting both cookies and a six-pack: if someone chooses to eat cookies, they prefer cookies to the six-pack. The dissatisfaction comes from wanting to hold both simultaneously. The resolution is either to eat fewer cookies (want less) or to trade the cookies for the six-pack (pay the cost). Applied to the roofing owner: he can either want less growth ($100M), or consciously pay the cost required (short-term profit loss, talent investment, reduced side-project stimulation).

Alex Hormozi described his own progression through hiring levels: his first $50k-per-year employee felt transformative compared to minimum-wage labor. Then his first six-figure hire made the $50k employee look limited. Each successive level ($250k, $500k, $1M, multi-million) revealed a new capability category that the previous level could not have supplied. The roofing owner at $6M is, in Hormozi's framing, at the entry point of what Hormozi calls the 'who game' — graduating from process execution hires to judgment-and-leadership hires.

Alex Hormozi referenced Warren Buffett's hypothetical 20-punch-card investment rule (each investor gets exactly 20 lifetime punches, can never sell, never gets more) to illustrate that artificial scarcity raises decision quality. Hormozi mapped this to entrepreneurial seasons: a founder who accepts that they have only 4–5 large bets available will be far more selective about which businesses they pour energy into than one who treats opportunities as unlimited.

practices.text: When evaluating a side business or investment, ask whether it actively changes what you do each day. If it does not change anything about your daily workflow, it is not a distraction. If you find yourself checking it constantly or optimizing it, treat it as a distraction and restructure or exit.

To preserve growth momentum while protecting family time, identify whether you value short-term profit or short-term family time more, then sacrifice the less-valued one deliberately rather than letting the business drift and sacrificing both by accident.

Keep passive investments (real estate, REITs, funds) in structures where competent partners manage them without requiring founder involvement. Do not make passive capital active by injecting personal optimization time unless you are prepared to treat it as a full active business.
